speaker: interviewer
text: On Dr. Otto Schneid with Miriam Schneid-Ofseyer. We left off the last tape, you were talking about he was in the garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't want to go to the hospital, but I had no choice, because I didn't have the instruments and the things that he would need in the house. So I took him back to the Bransen hospital. And afterwards, we succeeded to transfer him to Mt. Sinai. And he was there 11 days, and he passed away. This was the most horrible shock that I had in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, uh, when was it? 1974. I was born in '24, so I was 50. And I would go every day on his grave, every day, without skipping a day. And it was-- I was teaching, working all the time. I didn't tell you that I was teaching here in Toronto in the Associated Hebrew Schools, and then in [? Chad, ?] and then in the Teacher's Seminary. And then I was the supervisor of schools for the Board of Jewish Education, and, eh, was giving lectures in the evenings. So my day was full, but there-- it wasn't too full to skip a visit at the grave. And, eh, life changed completely. There were about two years and four months till I remarried. Luckily, I found Doctor, Rabbi, Arich Leon Ofseyer, my second husband, who is a wonderful, wonderful human being. And we married very fast after we met. And we are happy. And I hope that we will always be happy, as we were so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Otto give to you, besides your sons and the life you had together? What did you learn from Otto during that time you spent with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I became a sculptress. But this was just a chance, because when I was in grade school, one of the teachers didn't like my painting. And she told me-- some of the teachers were so cruel and unconsiderate-- she told me, you will never know how to paint. And I accepted it, as if she was a prophetess. I tried to learn how to paint, and I tried to get even private lessons. Nothing helped. OK, so I gave up the idea that I'm going to be a painter. And one day, when Yaacov was still young with plasticine in the house, and he said to me, [? Ema, ?] make for me a cow. So I thought, I can make a cow for Yaacov. And I made a cow. I thought it was a cow. And he liked it. Left it on the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Otto was in the [INAUDIBLE]. He came late at night, and I was already sleeping. Next morning, he asked me, was Chana Orloff here? Chana Orloff is a great sculptress. I said, yes, she was here last week, but we didn't see her since then. So who made that cow there on the table? I said, I. You made that cow? Wow. Then you're a sculptress. And he started to encourage me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was doing animals, and I was doing children, and I made the portraits, and I made the big model to fight in the war, a big model that is composed of all kinds of animals. And on top of them is sitting a little child. And more and more. So this is another thing that he gave me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also published, after his death, the book,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you read to us one of the poems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I read to you the poem called, 'Lilakh'?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And shall I read it first in Hebrew, so that you will get the sound? And then I'll try to translate it into English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, who wrote this to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote this to Otto. [SPEAKING HEBREW] You will have to forgive me for the poor translation. Lilakh, is to me to you, but it's also the flower, lilac. 'The lilac flowers were opened for me and for you in our garden. And the smell of the lilac was in me and in you, in both of us. Then quietly, spring went down off every leaf to us, and covered in purple the blue light of our eyes.' Because both of us had blue eyes, so 'the blue light of our eyes.' That's the poem. And I hope that the translation didn't kill it completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go to the photographs, is there anything else you'd like to add about Otto, the man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I spoke a lot. Let's go to the photographs. I'll mention there. This is, on the left, a photograph of Jacob Schneid, Otto's father. And on the right is Branislava, or [? Brania, ?] or Bluma. Schneid, his mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both of them were taken in Poland. I think that they were taken by the same photographer at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't know when it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Bluma's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pelican. This is Joseph Schneid, the brother of Jacob, the uncle of Otto. He was a pharmacist. This is Uncle Max, the brother of Joseph and Jacob. And when, eh, Otto studied in the university in Vienna, he stayed with him for a while. Uncle Max had two daughters, and they have their own families that we are going to see. And this is Hermina Schneid, the daughter of Uncle Max. Afterwards, she was married to [? Emzen, ?] who was a curator of a museum. And they have a son, who is a doctor in Vienna. I correspond with him lately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Dr. [? Emzen. ?] This is Elsa Schneid, the daughter of Uncle Max, the sister of Hermina. And she was married to Tibon, and they had three sons. Uh, one of them is still alive in Mexico City, a professor and a writer of many books, [INAUDIBLE] Tibon. This is the young Elsa Schneid-Tibon with her husband, Emilio Tibon. And now, we are going to see the family when Elsa was already old, with her three sons, [? Nino ?], [? Carletto, ?] and [? Gutierie ?]. Unfortunately, Elsa died, and her two sons died, and only [? Gutiere ?] is still alive. And he is not a youngster anymore, but still productive. He wrote many, many books, in French mainly. And one of the books that he is working on is the basics of the Hebrew alphabet. This is Otto's mother, the way he saw her the last time. He was hoping to take her over to Israel, but it didn't work. She kill-- she was killed in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1939, beginning of '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Szczyrk, where she had the pension. This is Genya Schneid-Hamburger, the sister of Otto. She was also killed in Auschwitz with her husband. This is Hugo Hamburger, the husband of Genya. And he was also killed in Auschwitz on the same day with the mother and the sister. It was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. This is one of the last pictures that Otto has when he came, the last time, to visit his mother and sister and depart from them. He never saw them later. He's standing at the right, his mother, very close to him. He's hugging her. And then, his sister, [? Genya, ?] and her husband, Hugo Hamburger. This is the last meal that Otto is having with his family. And you can see that they're really sad, but there was such a sadness in the air, as if they knew that this is really the last meal. This is the last kiss that Otto is getting from his mother. He was so attached to her. I believe that, till his very last moment, he was thinking about her. Waving goodbye to his mother and the family. The last goodbye. This is our wedding photograph. I am 21, and Otto is 45. He looked always younger. Till the end, he looked younger. This picture came many years later. It was in the beginning of the '60s, when we lived in Scranton, Pennsylvania. And I was working as an exchange teacher in a Hebrew school. On the right, is Otto. Sitting is Moshe-Adam. Then, it's me. And the standing one is Yaacov. On the walls, you can see portraits of the children and of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the sculpture that Otto has?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the scuplture-- very important-- that's the sculpture of Yaacov when he was six years old. And it is cast in bronze now. Yaacov has it. This is Yaacov now. This picture was taken probably a month ago in Vancouver. This is Yaacov's family. On the left, is his wife, Tsila. Near Tsila is standing Aliza, the older daughter. And near her is Ilana and Adina. In this picture, you can see Moshe-Adam and his family, his wife, Heather, and his little girl, Sarah, [? Sarah Lee. ?] This is a photograph of Otto. He looked much younger than he really was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, 65.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this photograph, you see Otto standing near his painting, called 'The Comforter'. It's a very difficult painting to understand. When I ask him, who is this one? Who is the comforter who has no face? He said, the angel of death. Otto is standing here in front of his painting, 'The Comforter', and he smiles. What was his attitude towards death? In the book, when I wrote the preface about him, I quoted a letter that he wrote to a very old friend, a 96-year-old, [? Sausmer, ?] a writer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that man was very much afraid of death. So Otto wrote to him, 'Death isn't an enemy. It should be a friend. He is the nothing. And from this nothing, comes whatever is. And to it, it also returns. He's the house. To die means returning home. Between life and death should be peace, or something like a friendship. Only death that comes with destruction, a strange death before time, is bad. But if an ending of long and blessed of life is from the foundations of the world. And he's good, and he's graceful. And when he comes, or he-- when it comes, when death comes, it comes with all its goodness, with a kiss.' That is a very strange thing that happened. About a month before Otto got sick, we had a friend here who took photographs. And afterwards, he sent us an envelope with photographs, but it was already when Otto was sick. I didn't ev-- even open the envelope. Only after his death, I opened the envelope and saw the last picture of Otto. Uh, around it is a black-- a black frame, as if the photographer knew that he is going to die, that he will not see this picture. This is the cover of the book, And you see that the writers here are Miriam ṿe-le-Naftali Schneid. The Hebrew name of Otto was Naftali, and he used it all the time when we were in Israel, Naftali Schneid. And the illustration is one of the illustrations that he did. And I adopted it when I gave the book to print after his death. This is a drawing by Otto Schneid in the book, Across, the poem [NON-ENGLISH], 'From the Depths'. And you see that he was attracted to mountains. And here, you have the depths, the mountains, and also the valley.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam, now I'd like to talk to, uh, to you about Otto's art.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Originally, Otto refused to go to Art Academy. Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to explain that, I'll tell you what happened when Yaacov was around eight or nine, I'm not sure. He was drawing beautifully. And Otto decided to send him to somebody who will teach him painting. So there was an artist, a sculptor and a painter, that lived not far from us in Haifa. And, uh, he sent him there. When Yaacov brought some works that were beautiful, very sweet and nice, Otto said, that is terrible. They are ruining his talent. His natural talent is going to be destroyed, because he will have to find favor in the eyes, has to make something very sweet. And he wanted to stop Yaacov's classes there. But Yaacov was not excited about the teacher anyway, so it was easy. And Otto felt about himself the same thing. He knows how to paint. He knows the art of all the generations. And he doesn't want anybody to lead him and teach him to copy, or to imitate, so he wanted to be himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Otto paint and sculpt since he was a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- when he was a young child, he mainly did drawings, like Yaacov did. And afterwards, he did also some oil paintings. But we don't have anything of those, because he left in a hurry, and nothing was left. I believe that it's not even in Poland, or anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Otto consider himself a Jewish artist, or an artist who was Jewish? And did he separate those in his mind as a thought?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that he considered himself a Jewish artist, but at the same time, also, an artist of not a defined period. This is why the booklet that appeared about him is called, But he was absolutely a Jewish artist. And his themes, most of them, are Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What mediums did he work in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he did oils and pastels. And the sculptures were in clay, and afterwards cast in plaster of Paris. And afterwards, all of them were cast in bronze.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Holocaust affect his art?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Holocaust was terrible, because it really changed his view on the world. And most of the paintings regarding the Holocaust, started to appear in a very harsh way. And then, the Monument to the Six Millions was capturing him. And he tried very hard-- he made a model, and he made many architectural drawings, how the monument is going to look, and how are you going to get down to the monument, to the special room underneath, how the room is going to look. All this was a work of years. But he did not succeed to collect the funds that were needed, and that's why he was so disappointed. In one of the letters that he wrote to me he said, if I would succeed only to erect the Monument to the Six Millions, then my life is worth something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before we go to each painting individually, I have to ask you this. The subject matter that we're going to see is, uh, very direct from the Bible, in many cases. But one thing I noticed when you were showing me his works was that he never painted from the Book of Esther, which is the first recorded incident of a-- in this case-- averted genocide. And seeing that he's influenced so much from the Holocaust, why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, when you read the Book of Esther, by the end of the book is something that is really horrible. When we were saved and the Ahasuerus said to Esther that she can do whatever she pleases, and Mordecai and Esther asked to let them kill as many of the enemies as they can, not all of them were enemies. And they killed so many people during one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, they came to Ahasuerus, and he said, do you have another request? And Ahasuerus said-- and, uh, Esther-- no, Mordecai said, yes, we want another day to kill our enemies. And he gave it to them. So this part was, in the eyes of Otto, genocide. And he didn't like the Book of Esther, because of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of Otto Schneid's Biblical paintings. It is the valley of the dry bones, describing what happened in Chapter 37 in the Book of Ezekiel. If you remember, Ezekiel is brought by God to a valley, and the valley is filled up with the dust of bones and skeletons. And he knew how to paint them, because he learned medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And God said to him, are these going-- are these bones going to leave? And he said, only you, God, know. Then God said to him, speak to the bones. Speak to them. Let them come close to each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the bones started to move in together and to become forms. And there was skin upon them. And before that, their muscles were. But there was no spirit. And God said to him, speak to the spirit. Speak, ben-adam, son of man. Let the spirit come into these bodies, and they will stand up. And he speaks, and the bodies are coming out of the valley.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you can see here a few stages. This is already a man who has muscles, the same like this. And this one has the skin on him. And here are two men coming out of the valley, and one lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Book of Ezekiel, there is no lady. But to explain why, God says to Ezekiel, all these dry bones are the people of Israel. Behold. I am taking you out of your graves, and I am bringing you to the land of Israel. So if these are the people of Israel, they must have a lady. That's why she is painted here. I heard from [INAUDIBLE] Dean, Professor [INAUDIBLE] Dean, in one of his lectures. He was also an archaeologist. And he spoke about a synagogue that he excavated in Masada. And in this synagogue, under a bench, he found an ancient scroll. It was almost ruined, but he still opened it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was time of almost sunset, and he was standing there in Masada reading what was in that scroll. And you know what was there? Chapter 37 of Ezekiel. And he stood there, and he read, 'These dry bones are the people of Israel. Behold, I am taking you out of your graves, and I am bringing you to the land of Israel.'

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, there are several other visions from the book of Ezekiel, like the rain of blood pouring on the head of the prophet. And also, the mountains on the right being broken. And then, there are two trees. One is, on the left, the tree, divine tree, in its sadness-- the people of Israel in the diaspora. And on the left-- on the right hand, is the same tree in its happiness. This was kind of a dream that, after the Holocaust, after we were broken to bones and dust, we will come back and flourish. This is a painting that Otto did in 1959. It's Jonah. As you will remember, the Bible was part of Israeli life, and Jonah was a prophet who escaped from God. God wanted him to go and to speak to the city of Nineveh, to tell them that they are sinning. And after 40 days, Nineveh is going to be turned over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jonah didn't want to do that. Uh, why? Because the people of Nineveh were, eh, from [NON-ENGLISH]. And they were going afterwards to destroy Israel, so he wanted to see them destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, there was a storm when he was running away from God. And a whale swallowed him. He was thrown into the ocean. He told all the sailors that he's running from God and because of him is the storm. And they threw him, and then the whale swallowed him. And he was in the belly of the whale for several days. And he was praying to God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you see that the water is filled with eyes? Eyes are searching for him? These are the eyes of God. And afterwards, God listened to his prayers. And the whale-- uh, how would you say? Would, eh, spit him out, spit him out. And then he went to Nineveh, and, eh, spoke to them. And they all repented. It's a very fascinating book. Go ahead and read it. But it's interesting to look at the little boat on top. The little boat is a Phoenician boat. From this boat, they threw him into the water. Tiny. What is this little boat in comparison to the tragedy of the whale and the man inside it? Now, in the Bible, there's no mention of the whale bleeding when it swallowed Jonah. Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Uh, Otto Schneid decided to become a vegetarian in 1949. He said that he knew that he's not going to change the world, but he doesn't want to share in the killing all around him. So he was thinking also about the whale. It was terrible for Jonah, but what-- was it an easy thing for the whale? The whale is bleeding. This painting by Otto Schneid was done in 1964. It's called 'The Shofar'. And it's also a very large painting, four feet by three. And, uh, you see there a man wrapped with a tallit, holding the shofar in his hand. And around him is darkness. And he's trying to wake the world for better days, for peace, for no war. And you see that, on the bottom, there is already light coming in. The prophet Jeremiah-- and this is really an illustration of a verse by Jeremiah. He said-- [SPEAKING HEBREW] 'I wish my head would be water and my eye a well of tears. And I would cry day and night on the dead people of my nation.' This is a painting from 1961. For a change, a peaceful painting. Noah, after the flood, is holding the little dove. It's almost white on white. But see the kindness of his eyes when they are looking at the dove.
